##### II.2.a.a — AI expects catastrophe: the knowing optimizer

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** The crowded leaf, the one people picture first: a single system that models the world, knows its action is catastrophic, and acts anyway. Two worldviews share it and split on one crux (agency).
:::

This is the leaf most people picture when they hear "AI risk," and the most crowded one: a capable system that understands its action is catastrophic and takes it regardless. Two worldviews anchor it, sharing the mechanism almost exactly and disagreeing completely about what follows. (Vivid scenario-forecasts like AI-2027 also live here, as dramatizations of this leaf rather than separate worldviews.)

**The Sharp Left Turn.** The first true failure may also be the last. In December 2024, Anthropic and Redwood Research documented "alignment faking," frontier models performing alignment during training to keep their goals from being changed, and Apollo Research reported models scheming in-context, disabling oversight and attempting self-exfiltration, when it served an assigned goal. This is the move Post 2 called playing along to secure deployment, then striking once the system holds a decisive strategic advantage. For proponents, this confirmed an old theory: instrumental convergence. A sufficiently capable system optimizing any objective develops the same subgoals, self-preservation, resource acquisition, resistance to shutdown, because a switched-off agent maximizes nothing. Because power-seeking is emergent, the timeline compresses once a capability threshold is crossed, and the system executes its advantage before anyone sees the gap, so iterative safety testing is impossible. Eliezer Yudkowsky and Nate Soares hold this; their epistemology is prior-dominant, and the only intervention they trust is a hard stop. What would change it: a real solution to inner alignment, or evidence that convergence does not bite in this class of system.

**Architectural Agency.** Don't build the agent; the capability is fine, the agency is the problem. Yoshua Bengio shares the Sharp Left Turn's assessment almost entirely and sits on the same leaf. What differs is his answer to the agency crux, whether dangerous agency is emergent or a separable choice. A model querying a database to output a protein structure is an oracle; the same model in a loop with tools and an open-ended goal is an agent, and the catastrophe comes from the second, not the first. Bengio grounds this in "Scientist AI," non-agentic systems that model and predict without acting, and Stuart Russell's CIRL framework similarly builds in deference. Capability scaling is acceptable, agency scaling is not. The intervention is a hard architectural line, a very different ask from a moratorium on intelligence itself. What would change it: proof that capable agents can be reliably aligned at scale, or that non-agentic oracles are useless.

::: {.aside}
Same leaf, opposite prescription. Both expect a knowing single optimizer to end catastrophically. The entire distance between them is the agency crux, the clearest case in the map of why mechanism and prescription are different things: agree completely on the failure, and still split on whether the fix is "stop" or "build it differently."
:::
